stem: Identify the arrow marked nerve:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The medial pectoral nerve arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus and innervates the pectoralis minor and part of the pectoralis major muscles. The nerve typically pierces the pectoralis minor before reaching the pectoralis major. In an anatomical image or dissection, the medial pectoral nerve can be identified as the nerve that passes through or near the pectoralis minor and then continues to supply the pectoralis major.



Other Options:
Option B: Lateral Pectoral Nerve: The lateral pectoral nerve arises from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus and primarily Innervates the pectoralls major muscle. Unlike the medial pectoral nerve, it does not pierce the pectoralis minor but rather passes above it. The lateral pectoral nerve is generally found more laterally and superiorly than the medial pectoral nerve
Option C: Long Thoracic Nerve: The long thoracic nerve innervates the serratus anterior muscle. It runs down the side of the thorax and can be seen on the surface of the serratus anterior. It is not associated with the pectoral muscles and is positioned more posteriorly and laterally compared to the pectoral nerves.
Option D: Thoracodorsal Nerve: The thoracodorsal nerve arises from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus and innervates the latissimus dorsi muscle. It is found on the posterior side of the thorax and is not involved with the pectoral muscles, making it distinct from the medlal pectoral nerve.

stem: Name artery behind the medial malleolus and in front of Achilles?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
Posterior tibial artery: The posterior tibial artery is located behind the medial malleolus and in front of the Achilles tendon. It runs posterior to the medial malleolus and can be palpated in this region.



Other Options:
Option B: Peroneal artery (also known as the fibular artery) is located along the lateral side of the leg and is not found behind the medial malleolus. It supplies the lateral compartment of the leg.
Option C: Anterior tibial artery runs down the anterior part of the leg, in front of the interosseous membrane, and becomes the dorsalis pedis artery at the ankle. It is not located behind the medial malleolus.
Option D: Dorsalis pedis artery: The dorsalis pedis artery is located on the dorsum (top) of the foot, not behind the medial malleolus.

stem: The covering of an omphalocele is derived from which layer?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
Amnion: The covering of an omphalocele is derived from the amnion. An omphalocele is a congenital defect where abdominal organs herniate through the umbilical ring into the base of the umbilical cord, and these herniated organs are covered by a membrane composed of amnion on the outside and peritoneum on the inside.



Other Options:
Option B: Chorion: The chorion is another fetal membrane, but it does not contribute to the covering of an omphalocele.
Option C: Mesoderm: The mesoderm is a germ layer involved in the formation of various tissues and organs but not in the direct covering of an omphalocele.
Option D: Endoderm: The endoderm forms the lining of the gastrointestinal tract and other internal structures but does not contribute to the covering of an omphalocele.

stem: A 45-year-old man with underlying DM on medication, history of being admitted for septic shock secondary to pneumonia. CT scan brain done due to poor GCS with incidental finding of suprasellar mass. This would result in which kind of field abnormality?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
Craniopharyngiomas are often located near the pituitary gland and the optic chiasm. A tumor in this area can compress the optic chiasm, which affects the crossing fibers of the optic nerves from the nasal (medial) fields of vision. This results in loss of vision in the temporal (lateral) fields of both eyes, known as bitemporal hemianopla.



Other Options:
Option A: Homonymous hemianopia: This type of vision loss occurs when there is damage to the optic tract or the visual cortex, leading to loss of vision in the same field of both eyes (either left or right). This is not typically associated with craniopharyngiomas.
Option C: Complete blindness: Complete blindness would occur with damage to both the optic nerves or the visual pathways at a more extensive level, not just from a craniopharyngioma affecting the optic chiasm.
Option D: Single vision field loss: This would imply a unilateral field loss, which is not characteristic of craniopharyngiomas. They usually affect the visual fields in a bitemporal manner due to their location affecting the optic chiasm.

stem: Which of the following prevents polyspermy?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
This is a key mechanism to prevent polyspermy. Upon sperm penetration, cortical granules in the oocyte release their contents, which modify the zona pellucida (the layer surrounding the oocyte). This change creates a barrier that prevents additional sperm from binding and entering the oocyte, ensuring that only one sperm fertilizes the egg.



Other Options:
Option A: Acrosome reaction: The acrosome reaction is the process by which the sperm releases enzymes to penetrate the zona pellucida of the oocyte. It is crucial for sperm to access the egg but does not prevent polyspermy.
Option C: Zona reaction: Incorrect. The zona reaction involves changes in the zona pellucida but is not the primary mechanism preventing polyspermy. It refers to the structural changes in the zona pellucida that occur after the cortical reaction.
Option D: Capacitation: Incorrect. Capacitation is a physiological process that sperm undergo to gain the ability to fertilize an egg. It involves changes to the sperm's membrane and is necessary for fertilization but does not prevent polyspermy.

stem: The nerve passing through the red arrow marked region has been lesioned. Which of the following will be the clinical presentation?
options: Loss of sensation in the mucosa of nasopharynx
Loss of sensation in maxillary area of face
Loss of sensation in jaw area
Loss of sensation in scalp and nose
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The superior orbital fissure is a crucial anatomical opening in the skull through which several important nerves pass. These Include the oculomotor nerve (CN III), trochlear nerve (CN IV), abducens nerve (CN VI), and the ophthalmic division (V1) of the trigeminal nerve (CN V), The ophthalmic division (VI) of the trigeminal nerve provides sensory innervation to the scalp, forehead, upper eyelid, nose, and the mucosa of the frontal sinuses. If the nerve passing through the superior orbital fissure is lesioned, the patient would experience loss of sensation in the scalp and nose due to the impairment of the ophthalmic nerve (VI).



Other Options:
Option A-Loss of sensation in the mucosa of nasopharynx: Incorrect: This sensation is typically provided by branches of the maxillary nerve (V2) and glossopharyngeal nerve (CN (X), not by the ophthalmic nerve (V1), which passes through the superior orbital fissure.
Option B-Loss of sensation in maxillary area of face: Incorrect: The maxillary division (V2) of the trigeminal nerve provides sensation to the maxillary region, but it exits the skull through the foramen rotundum, not the superior orbital fissure.
Option C-Loss of sensation in jaw area: Incorrect: The mandibular division (V3) of the trigeminal nerve provides sensation to the jaw, but it exits through the foramen. ovale, not the superior orbital fissure.

stem: Identify the labeling correctly:
options: A- Superior vena cava, B-Ascending aorta, C- Pulmonary trunk, D- Descending aorta
A- Ascending aorta, B- Descending aorta, C- Pulmonary trunk, D- Superior vena cava
A-Ascending aorta, B- Superior vena cava, C- Descending aorta, D- Pulmonary trunk
A- Ascending aorta, B- Superior Vena Cava, C-Pulmonary trunk, D- Descending aorta
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
A- Superior vena cava, B- Ascending aorta, C- Pulmonary trunk, D- Descending aorta
A. Superior vena cava: The superior vena cava is a large vein that carries deoxygenated blood from the upper half of the body to the right atrium of the heart. It is located anterior and slightly to the right of the ascending aorta.
B. Ascending aorta: The ascending aorta arises from the left ventricle of the heart and is the initial portion of the aorta that moves upward before arching.
C. Pulmonary Trunk: The pulmonary trunk is the vessel that arises from the right ventricle and bifurcates into the left and right pulmonary arteries, which carry deoxygenated blood to the lungs. It is positioned anterior to the ascending aorta and the superior vena cava.
D. Descending aorta: The descending aorta is the continuation of the aortic arch that runs downwards through the thorax and abdomen, supplying oxygenated blood to the body.



Incorrect Options:
Option B, C, D Incorrectly label the structures.

stem: A 14-year-old boy presents with jaundice, muscle stiffness, tremors, behavioral changes, hepatosplenomegaly, and Kayser-Fleischer rings. Which test is definitive for the diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The clinical presentation suggests Wilson's disease, which is diagnosed by serum ceruloplasmin test.



Other Options:
Option A, C, and D are less preferred to detect Wilson's disease.

stem: A 55 year old female patient presents with xanthomas on eyes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) is an inherited disorder characterized by high levels of low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol in the blood. Xanthomas, which are deposits of cholesterol in the skin or tendons, are a hallmark of this condition.



Other Options:
Options B, C, and D do not present with tendon xanthomas.

stem: A patient with multiple tendon xanthomas is found to have a serum cholesterol level of $400~mg/dL$ and an LDL level of $230~mg/dL.$ What is the most likely defect?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
The most likely defect in a patient with multiple tendon xanthomas, elevated serum cholesterol levels, and LDL levels is an LDL receptor defect. LDL levels in the blood remain high because the liver and other cells cannot effectively remove LDL from the bloodstream. This leads to elevated cholesterol levels and the formation of xanthomas due to the accumulation of cholesterol in tissues.



Other Options:
Options A, C, and D do not present with the given findings.

stem: A neonate presents with poor feeding and vomiting, cataract and acidosis with a positive Benedict's test on urine and negative urinary glucose. Which enzyme is likely defective?
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Galactose-1-phosphate uridyl transferase (GALT) deficiency is the most likely cause of the clinical presentation of poor feeding. vomiting, cataracts, acidosis, a positive Benedict's test, and negative urinary glucose.



Other Options:
Options B, C, and D are not responsible for causing this patient's condition.

stem: A patient's friend has requested a teleconsultation. The friend sent a message mentioning that urinary Coproporphyrin is positive and still waiting for the results of other Investigations. What is the probable cause?
subject: Medicine, FMT
explanation:
Solution
Lead poisoning is associated with elevated levels of urinary coproporphyrin, which is a type of porphyrin. Lead inhibits key enzymes in the heme synthesis pathway, particularly ALA dehydratase and ferrochelatase. This inhibition causes a buildup of precursors, Including coproporphyrin, In the urine. Elevated urinary coproporphyrin is a characteristic finding in cases of lead poisoning and is used as a diagnostic marker.



Other Options:
Options B, C, and D are Incorrect, as explained above.

stem: A 33 year old patient presents with dermatitis, dementia, and cognitive decline. Which micronutrient deficiency is most likely responsible?
subject: Medicine, Biochemistry, Dermatology, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
The deficiency of Niacin causes pellagra, and it is characterized by 3 D's:
Diarrhoea
Dementia
Dermatitis



Other Options:
Options D, B, and Care Incorrect, as explained above.

stem: A patient's X-ray shows cardiomegaly, and they present with a 'floppy baby syndrome, hypotonia, macroglossia, and hepatomegaly. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics, Biochemistry, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
The given clinical features are suggestive of Pompe's disease, which is a lysosomal storage disorder and a glycogen storage disorder.



Other Options:
Options B, C, and D do not cause floppy baby syndrome.

stem: A patient presents to the emergency room with severe abdominal pain, and serum triglyceride levels are 1700 mg/dL. What is the most likely underlying defect?
subject: Biochemistry, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The patient's presentation suggests familial chylomicronaemia syndrome, and it is caused by an Apo-Cll defect.



Other Options:
Options A, B, and D do not cause familial chylomicronaemia.

stem: Which micronutrient deficiency causes anemia, bleeding guns, and Frenkel's white line on X-ray?
subject: Medicine, Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Vitamin C influences the absorption of iron, so in case of vitamin C deficiency, iron absorption will be hampered, causing anemla. Other manifestations of scurvy include bleeding and swollen gums. The white line of Frenkel is also seen on the X-ray.



Other Options:
Options A, B, and D do not present with the given clinical condition.

stem: A 37 year old male patient with high triglycerides esterified with long-chain fatty acids presents with fatigue and muscle blopsy shows fat vacuoles. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
Carnitine deficiency manifests with fatigue, and muscle biopsy shows fat vacuoles.



Other Options:
Options B, C, and D do not show fat vacuoles in muscle biopsy.

stem: Which of the following is deficient in a patient recently diagnosed with phenylketonuria with normal phenylalanine hydroxylase levels?
subject: Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Phenylketonuria with normal phenylalanine hydroxylase can be caused by a deficiency of dihydrobiopterin reductase, which helps in the conversion of dihydrobiopterin to tetrahydrobiopterin. This leads to the failure of the conversion of phenylalanine to tyrosine.



Other Options:
Options A, B, and C do not cause phenylketonurla.

stem: A 6-year-old girl was washing her toy with detergent containing rotenone, Her caretaker noticed her in an unconscious state. Which is Inhibited by the above chemical?
subject: Biochemistry, FMT
explanation:
Solution
Rotenone inhibits NADH dehydrogenase.



Other Options:
Options B, C, and D are not inhibited by rotenone.

stem: A 45-year-old patient with a known case of homocystinuria presents with joint pain and weakness. Which vitamin is required for the treatment?
subject: Medicine, Biochemistry, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Homocystinuria is often due to a deficiency in the enzyme cystathionine B-synthase, which requires Vitamin Bó as a cofactor. Supplementing with Vitamin B6 can help reduce homocysteine levels and alleviate symptoms like joint pain and weakness.



Other Options:
Options B, C, and D are not preferred in the management of this patient.

stem: A mother brings her 14-year-old mentally retarded son to the clinic, complaining of joint pain and an irresistible urge for the boy to bite his fingers and lips. Serum uric acid is elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Biochemistry, Pediatrics, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
Lesch-Nyhan syndrome is caused by a deficiency of the enzyme hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase (HGPRT). This enzyme is essential for the salvage pathway of purines, and its deficiency leads to the accumulation of uric acid in the body, causing hyperuricemia. The patient also presents with self-mutilating behavior.



Other Options:
Options B, C, and D do not show the given findings.

stem: Most things that taste sour are acidic solutions like lemon juice or organic acids. This 'sour' sensation is caused by hydrogen lons (chemical symbol: H+). Acidic substances release hydrogen ions when they come into contact with water. Sour taste is perceived by which receptors?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
TRPP3 (also known as PKD2L1) is a receptor involved in the perception of sour taste. It detects acidic (sour) stimuli by responding to the presence of protons (H+ ions) in the food.



Other Options:
Option A-T1R1: This receptor is primarily associated with detecting umami taste, often in combination with T1R3. It does not play a role in sour taste perception.
Option BT1R2: TIR2, along with T1R3, is involved in the perception of sweet taste. It is not responsible for detecting sour taste.
Option C-T1R3: T1R3 pairs with TIR1 for umami taste and with T1R2 for sweet taste. It is not involved in the perception of sour taste.

stem: The resting membrane potential is the result of the movement of several different ion species through various lon channels and transporters (uniporters, cotransporters, and pumps) in the plasma membrane. Which lon is most responsible for the resting membrane potential (RMP)?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
Potassium lons are the primary contributors to the resting membrane potential. The RMP is mainly determined by the K+ gradient across the cell membrane, as potassium ions move out of the cell through potassium leak channels. The loss of positively charged K+ lons from the cell creates a negative charge inside the cell relative to the outside, establishing the RMP. which is typically around -70mV in neurons.



Other Options:
Option B: Calcium (Ca2+); Calcium lons play a crucial role in various cellular processes, including muscle contraction and neurotransmitter release. However, their influence on the RMP is relatively minimal compared to potassium. The primary role of calcium in relation to membrane potential is more related to action potentials and signal transduction rather than establishing the RMP.
Option C: Sodium (Na+); Sodium ions also contribute to the membrane potential, but they are less influential than potassium in establishing the resting membrane potential. The sodium-potassium pump ( Na+/K+ ATPase) maintains the gradient by actively transporting 3 Na lons out of the cell and 2K+ ions into the cell, which helps in stabilizing the RMP. However, the resting membrane potential is primarily determined by the K+ gradient.
Option D: Chloride (Cl-): Chloride ions contribute to the membrane potential by balancing the charge, but they are not the primary determinant of the resting membrane potential. Chloride ions typically follow the electrochemical gradients established by other ions like potassium and sodium.

stem: The taste buds are bulb-shaped structures responsible for taste perception, located within the lingual papillae and in the surface mucosa of the soft palate, oropharynx, epiglottis, and upper esophagus. They are formed by basal cells and thin taste bud cells that converge into the taste pit. Which tongue papillae does not have taste buds?
subject: Physiology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
Filiform papillae are the only type of papillae on the tongue that do not contain taste buds. Their primary role is to provide a rough surface that helps with food manipulation and the sensation of texture, rather than taste perception.



Other Options:
Option A Fungiform: Fungiform papillae are mushroom-shaped and are found on the anterior part of the tongue. They contain taste buds that are sensitive to sweet, salty, sour, and umami flavors.
Option C- Circumvallate: Circumvallate papillae are large, dome-shaped structures located at the back of the tongue in a V- shaped arrangement. Each papilla is surrounded by a trench and contains multiple taste buds, mainly sensitive to bitter flavors.
Option D-Foliate: Follate papillae are ridged structures located on the sides of the tongue. They contain taste buds and are particularly sensitive to sour flavors.

stem: Caffeine is the most widely used central nervous system (CNS) stimulant in the world. It has numerous pharmacological and physiological effects, including cardiovascular, respiratory, renal, and smooth muscle effects, as well as effects on mood, memory, alertness, and physical and cognitive performance. Caffeine impairs with sleep by which of the following:
options: Activates histamine release and prevents sleep
subject: Physiology, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Caffeine works by blocking adenosine receptors. Adenosine is a neurotransmitter that promotes sleep by slowing down nerve activity. By blocking adenosine, caffeine prevents the brain from sensing sleepiness, thereby promoting wakefulness.



Other Options:
Option B Activates locus coeruleus and wakefulness: The locus coeruleus is a nucleus in the brainstem Involved in arousal and wakefulness. While it does play a role in promoting wakefulness, caffeine's primary mechanism of action is not through direct activation of the locus coeruleus.
Option C- No role in maintaining wakefulness: Caffeine is well-known for its role in maintaining wakefulness by counteracting sleep-promoting mechanisms in the brain.
Option D-Activates histamine release and prevents sleep: Histamine is involved in promoting wakefulness, but caffeine does not directly cause histamine release. Its effect on sleep is primarily through adenosine receptor blockade rather than histamine pathways.

stem: Select the correct option regarding the function of receptors?
options: Ruffini corpuscle - Sustained pressure and stretching
subject: Physiology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
Pacinian corpuscles are specialized receptors that detect vibration and deep pressure. They are rapidly adapting and are found deep in the dermis and subcutaneous tissue.



Other Options:
Option A - Ruffini corpuscle - Light touch: Ruffini corpuscles are sensitive to sustained pressure and stretching of the skin, particularly detecting skin stretch and contributing to the perception of object manipulation.
Option B- Merkel cells - Vibration: Merkel cells are responsible for detecting light touch and pressure, providing fine tactile discrimination. They are slow-adapting receptors located in the skin and mucosa.
Option C-Meissner's corpuscle - Sustained pressure and stretching: Meissner's corpuscles are sensitive to light touch and changes in texture. They are rapidly adapting receptors located in areas such as fingertips and lips, where precise touch is essential.

stem: A 20 year old male patient had a road traffic accident causing his right limb amputation. He is experiencing phantom limb pain after the amputation of right limb. What is observed on a PET scan in a patient with phantom limb pain?
options: Expansion of right somatosensory fibers for the hand into neighboring areas
Neighboring fibers of the right somatosensory area extending into the right-hand area
subject: Physiology, Medicine, Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
After amputation, the brain undergoes cortical reorganization. In this case, the neighboring somatosensory fibers that previously represented adjacent body parts extend into the region that formerly represented the right hand. This reorganization is associated with the perception of phantom limb pain.



Other Options:
Option A - Expansion of right somatosensory fibers for the hand into neighboring areas: This option suggests that the somatosensory fibers specifically responsible for the right hand expand into neighboring regions.. However, It is not the fibers themselves that expand; rather, it is the cortical representation that changes.
Option C - Expansion of right sensory cortex: This option implies a general enlargement of the right sensory cortex. However, the expansion is not global; rather, it involves the neighboring cortical areas invading the region corresponding to the amputated limb.
Option D - Expansion of left sensory cortex: The left sensory cortex is responsible for processing sensory Input from the right side of the body, but this option inaccurately describes the phenomenon. The reorganization occurs in the sensory cortex corresponding to the amputated limb (in this case, the right cortex, which processes left-sided sensations).

stem: Which of the following statements is incorrect regarding Samter's triad?
options: It is only triggered by aspirin
Other NSAIDs with COX-1 blocking ability can also trigger symptoms
It is characterized by asthma, nasal polyps, and aspirin sensitivity
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine, ENT
explanation:
Solution
This statement is incorrect because Samter's triad, also known as aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD), cari be triggered not only by aspirin but also by other nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) that inhibit cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1). These include ibuprofen, naproxen, and others that block COX-1. Therefore, patients with Samter's triad must avoid all COX-1 inhibiting NSAIDs, not just aspirin.



Other Options:
Option B-Other NSAIDs with COX-1 blocking ability can also trigger symptoms (Correct Statement): This is a correct statement. In Samter's triad, the use of NSAIDs that inhibit COX-1 can trigger symptoms like asthma exacerbation, nasal congestion, and other respiratory Issues due to their Impact on the arachidonic acid pathway, leading to increased production of leukotrienes.
Option c-H is characterized by asthma, nasal polyps, and aspirin sensitivity (Correct Statement): This is a correct statement. Samter's triad is defined by the presence of three key features: asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps, and sensitivity to aspirin or other COX-1 Inhibiting NSAIDS.
Option D-It involves overproduction of leukotrienes (Correct Statement): Explanation: This is a correct statement. In Samter's triad, the inhibition of COX-1 by aspirin or NSAIDs leads to the overproduction of leukotrienes, which are inflammatory mediators that contribute to the symptoms of asthma, nasal polyps, and other respiratory issues.

stem: A 37 year old male has been prescribed aprepitant, a drug used to prevent late-onset chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting. Identify the mechanism through which this drug acts?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Mechanism of Action: Aprepitant is a selective antagonist of the neurokinin-1 (NK-1) receptor. By blocking the NK-1 receptors, aprepitant inhibits the action of substance P, a neuropeptide involved in the emetic pathway. This helps to prevent nausea and vomiting, particularly the late-onset type associated with chemotherapy.
Use: It is effective in managing delayed chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV) when used in combination with other antiemetic agents.



Other Options:
Option B-NK-1 Agonist: This is incorrect because aprepitant is not an agonist but an antagonist of the NK-1 receptor. An NK-1 agonist would stimulate the receptor, potentially increasing the risk of nausea and vomiting rather than preventing it.
Option C-NK-3 Antagonist: Aprepitant does not act on the NK-3 receptors. NK-3 receptors are another subtype of neurokinin receptors, and antagonists targeting these receptors are not used in the treatment of chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting.
Option D-NK-2 Antagonist: Aprepitant is not an NK-2 antagonist. NK-2 receptors are another subtype Involved in the emetic response, but aprepitant specifically targets the NK-1 receptors. NK-2 antagonists are not the primary agents used for preventing chemotherapy- induced nausea and vomiting.

stem: A 75 year old woman has been diagnosed with urinary incontinence which is triggered by coughing, sneezing. Which of the following drugs is appropriate for treating such a condition?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
Mechanism of Action: Tolterodine is an antimuscarinic agent that specifically targets muscarinic receptors in the bladder. It reduces involuntary bladder contractions by blocking these receptors, which can help in managing. symptoms of urinary incontinence, particularly urge incontinence.
Use in Stress Incontinence: While tolterodine is primarily used to treat urge incontinence, it can sometimes be beneficial in mixed incontinence cases where urgency is also a factor. However, the first-line treatment for pure stress Incontinence typically Involves non-pharmacological approaches like pelvic floor exercises. Pharmacologically, stress incontinence is less commonly treated with medications, but if there is an urgency component, tolterodine may be considered.



Other Options:
Option A-Ipratropium: Mechanism of Action: Ipratropium is an anticholinergic drug used mainly as a bronchodilator in the treatment of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and asthma. It has no role in the management of urinary incontinence.
Option C-Telenzepine: Mechanism of Action: Telenzepine is a selective M1 muscarinic receptor antagonist used mainly for reducing gastric acid secretion in conditions like peptic ulcer disease. It is not indicated for treating urinary Incontinence.
Option D-Tropicamide: Mechanism of Action: Tropicamide is an antimuscarinic drug used primarily as a mydriatic agent to dilate the pupil for ophthalmic examinations. It has no effect on bladder function and is not used for urinary incontinence.

stem: What is the inclusion criterion for starting bedaqulline in a patient presenting with multidrug-resistant (MDR) or rifampicin-resistant (RR) tuberculosis?
options: Resistance to both rifampicin and Isoniazid with mutations in the InhA and katG genes
Resistance to rifampicin but sensitivity to fluoroquinolones
Resistance to rifampicin and sensitivity to isoniazid
Resistance to both rifampicin and fluoroquinolones
subject: Medicine, Pharmacology, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Bedaquiline is typically used for treating multidrug-resistant (MDR) tuberculosis, which includes cases with resistance to both rifampicin and isoniazid. This includes strains with mutations in the InhA and katG genes, which are associated with isoniazid resistance. Bedaquiline is considered in such cases when other second-line drugs are not effective or suitable, providing a vital treatment option for MDR-TB.



Other Options:
Option B - Resistance to Rifampicin but Sensitivity to Fluoroquinolones: While resistance to rifampicin is a key factor in MDR-TB, the inclusion criterion for starting bedaquiline is more specific. Sensitivity to fluoroquinolones alone does not meet the criteria for bedaqulline use; it is usually considered in the broader context of multidrug-resistant TB, including resistance to both rifampicin and isoniazid.
Option C-Resistance to Rifampicin and Sensitivity to Isoniazid: Resistance to rifampicin and sensitivity to isoniazid would be classified as rifampicin-resistant TB rather than MDR-TB. Bedaquiline is typically used for MDR-TB, which involves resistance to both rifampicin and isoniazid, not just one.
Option D- Resistance to Both Rifampicin and Fluoroquinolones: Resistance to both rifampicin and fluoroquinolones suggests a more severe form of drug-resistant TB, potentially extensively drug-resistant (XDR-TB), Bedaquiline may still be used in such cases, but the initial criterion for starting it is based on MDR-TB. which includes resistance to both rifampicin and Isoniazid.

stem: Chelator contraindicated in Iron poisoning?
subject: Pharmacology, FMT
explanation:
Solution
Mechanism of Action: BAL (dimercaprol) is a chelator primarily used for the treatment of heavy metal poisoning, specifically arsenic, mercury, and lead. Use in Iron Poisoning: BAL is contraindicated in Iron poisoning because it is not effective for iron and can potentially displace Iron from its chelation sites, worsening the situation. It does not effectively bind Iron and could potentially complicate the clinical picture in iron toxicity.



Other Options:
Option A Calcium Disodium EDTA: Mechanism of Action: Calcium disodium EDTA (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid) is a chelator used for lead poisoning and other heavy metals. While it is not the first choice for iron poisoning, it is used in some cases of iron overload to a limited extent. Use in Iron Poisoning: Calcium disodium EDTA is not typically used for iron poisoning, but it is not contraindicated. It is more commonly used for lead and other heavy metal poisonings.
Option B-Deferoxamine: Mechanism of Action: Deferoxamine is a specific iron chelator that binds to free iron in the bloodstream and facilitates its excretion. Use in Iron Poisoning: Deferoxamine is the drug of choice for treating iron poisoning. It is effective in removing excess iron from the body and is specifically Indicated for iron overload conditions.
Option D-Penicillamine: Mechanism of Action: Pernicillamine is a chelator used primarily for copper poisoning (e.g., Wilson's disease) and can also be used for lead poisoning. Use in Iron Poisoning: Penicillamine is generally not used for iron poisoning, but it is not contraindicated. It is less effective compared to deferoxamine for Iron overload and is not the first choice..

stem: A 50 year old woman suffering from multiple myeloma is on a drug which is associated with increased risk of developing Herpes zoster infection. Identify the drug?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine, Microbiology, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
Mechanism of Action: Bortezomib is a proteasome inhibitor used in the treatment of multiple myeloma and mantle cell lymphoma. Association with Herpes Zoster: Bortezomib has been associated with an increased risk of reactivation of the varicella- zoster virus (VZV), leading to Herpes zoster infection. This is due to the immunosuppressive effects of bortezomib, which can impair the immune system's ability to keep latent infections in check. Prophylaxis: Patients receiving bortezomib are often prescribed antiviral prophylaxis (e.g., acyclovir) to reduce the risk of Herpes zoster reactivation.



Other Options:
Option B-Daratumumab: Mechanism of Action: Daratumumab is a monoclonal antibody that targets CD38 on plasma cells, used in the treatment of multiple myeloma. Infection Risk: While daratumumab is associated with an increased risk of respiratory Infections due to its Immunomodulatory effects, it is not specifically linked to a higher risk of Herpes zoster infection.
Option C-Lomitapide: Mechanism of Action: Lomitapide is a microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTP) inhibitor used to treat homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia, not multiple myeloma Relevance: Lomitapide is not associated with an increased risk of Herpes zoster, nor is it relevant in the context of multiple myeloma treatment.
Option D-Lenalidomide: Mechanism of Action: Lenalidomide is an immunomodulatory drug used in multiple myeloma. Infection Risk: Although lenalidomide is associated with an increased risk of neutropenia and Infections, it is not specifically associated with an increased risk of Herpes zoster Infection compared to bortezomib.

stem: Which of the following beta-adrenergic blockers used in the treatment of chronic heart failure (CHF) is known to prolong survival?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Mechanism of Action: Bisoprolol is a selective beta-1 adrenergic blocker. It works by decreasing the heart rate, reducing myocardial contractility, and lowering blood pressure, which reduces the workload on the heart. Prolonging Survival: Bisoprolol is one of the beta-blockers that has been proven to improve survival in patients with chronic heart failure (CHF). It is specifically Indicated in the management of CHF due to its ability to reduce mortality and hospitalization by counteracting the harmful effects of excessive sympathetic stimulation on the heart. Clinical Trials: The CIBIS-II trial (Cardiac Insufficiency Bisoprolol Study II) demoristrated a significant reduction in mortality and hospitalization rates in patients with heart failure treated with bisoprolol



Other Options:
Option B-Labetalol: Mechanism of Action: Labetalol is a non-selective beta-blocker with additional alpha-1 adrenergic blocking activity. Use in Heart Failure: Labetalol is primarily used for the management of hypertension and hypertensive emergencies rather than for chronic heart failure. It is not known to prolong survival in CHF and is not routinely used for this purpose.
Option C- Propranolol: Mechanism of Action: Propranolol is a non-selective beta-blocker that affects both beta-1 and beta-2 receptors. Use in Heart Failure: Propranolol is generally used for conditions such as hypertension, angina, and arrhythmias but is not commonly used in the management of chronic heart failure. It does not have evidence supporting a survival benefit in CHF patients.
Option D-Atenolol: Mechanism of Action: Atenolol is a selective beta-1 adrenergic blocker, similar to bisoprolol. Use in Heart Failure: Atenolol is less commonly used in chronic heart failure and has not shown a survival benefit in this population. It is more frequently used for managing hypertension and angina pectoris.

stem: Which drug acts on the rounded-up enzyme?
subject: Pharmacology, Medicine, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Aminocaproic acid is an antifibrinolytic agent that works by inhibiting plasminogen activation, thereby preventing the conversion of plasminogen to plasmin, which is responsible for fibrin clot breakdown. PAI-1 (Plasminogen Activator Inhibitor-1) inhibits tissue plasminogen activator (tPA), reducing plasmin formation. Aminocaproic acid effectively counteracts excessive fibrinolysis and stabilizes clots by preventing plasmin formation, indirectly related to the action of PAI-1.



Incorrect Options:
Option B-Tenecteplase: Tenecteplase is a fibrinolytic (thrombolytic) agent that works by directly activating plasminogen to plasmin, which breaks down fibrin clots. Tenecteplase does not act on PAI-1; instead, its action would be opposed by PAI-1 because PAI-1 inhibits tissue plasminogen activator (PA), reducing the activation of plasminogen.
Option C-Streptokinase: Streptokinase is another fibrinolytic agent that works by forming a complex with plasminogen, leading to the formation of plasmin and subsequent breakdown of clots. Like tenecteplase, streptokinase's action is not directly on PAI-1, but rather on plasminogen, and its activity can be inhibited by PAI-1.
Option D- Apixaban: Apixaban is an anticoagulant that works by directly inhibiting Factor Xa, an essential enzyme in the coagulation cascade. Apixaban does not interact with the fibrinolytic system or PAI-1, and its mechanism of action is unrelated to PAI-1 or plasminogen activation.

stem: A 35-year-old farmer presents with nausea, vomiting and diarrhea for a few days. He recently experienced retro-orbital pain with burning red eyes, fever with chills, calf tenderness, and joint pain. What is the most likely diagnosis?

Gi symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) are common
Myalgia/arthralgia are prominent in leptospirosis
Hence, the presentation is most consistent with leptospirosis

• Dengue usually has thrombocytopenia/bleeding tendency features
Incorrect

Gl symptoms can occur but are non-specific

stem: A 25 year fruit farmer presents with fever, respiratory difficulties, and neurological complications. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Nipah virus is a zoonotic virus that fruit farmers are particularly at risk of contracting due to its association with fruit bats. It presents with fever, respiratory difficulties, and severe neurological complications, including encephalitis, making it the most likely diagnosis in this case.



Other Options:
  • Option A: Ebola virus primarily causes hemorrhagic fever with symptoms like severe bleeding, multi-organ failure, and shock. While it can present with fever and some neurological complications, respiratory symptoms are not typical, and there is no specific association with fruit farming.
  • Option B: Meningitis causes inflammation of the meninges surrounding the brain and spinal cord, leading to fever, headache, neck stiffness, and neurological complications. However, respiratory symptoms are uncommon, and the condition is not linked to fruit farming.
  • Option D: Zika virus primarily causes mild symptoms like fever, rash, and joint pain, and is more famous for its complications during pregnancy, such as microcephaly in newborns. It does not commonly cause severe respiratory or neurological Issues, nor is it specifically associated with fruit farming.

stem: Which of the following vaccines is kept at the lowest level?

Solution

stem: A 25 year old mother delivers in a rural area under the guidance of a skilled care attendant. Which of the following is incorrect regarding the care provided by the skilled care attendant at birth?

Bathe the baby with warm water
Clear eyes with a sterile swab
subject: PSM, Pediatrics, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
This option is incorrect because immediate bathing can lead to hypothermia and is discouraged right after birth. Instead, the focus should be on keeping the baby warm and starting breastfeeding.



Other Options:
  • Option A: Early initiation of breastfeeding, ideally within the first hour of birth, is crucial for the baby's health. It helps with bonding, provides essential nutrients, and supports the infant's immune system.
  • Option B: Covering the baby's head and body Immediately after birth is important to prevent heat loss and maintain the baby's body temperature, especially in rural or resource-limited settings.
  • Option D: Clearing the baby's eyes with a sterile swab helps prevent eye infections, which is part of routine newbom care.

stem: A 48 year patient visits OPD for routine check up but does not understand the meaning of the doctor's language. Which type of barrier is this?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
The scenario describes a situation where the patient does not understand the meaning of the doctor's words, which likely stems from differences in cultural context or bellefs. This makes it a cultural barrier, where the patient's background influences their interpretation or understanding of the Information provided by the doctor.



Other Options:
  • Option B: Linguistic barriers occur when there is a difference in the language spoken by the patient and the healthcare provider. If the misunderstanding is due to language differences or poor comprehension of medical terminology, then the barrier Is linguistic. However, since the question suggests that the issue is related to understanding the meaning, and not necessarily the language itself, this is not the primary barrier in this case.
  • Option C: Psychological barriers are related to emotional or mental states, such as anxiety, fear, or mistrust, which may prevent effective communication. If the patient is unable to understand due to stress or other psychological factors, this could be a psychological barrier. However, the scenario described does not specifically indicate psychological Issues.
  • Option D: Environmental barriers are external factors such as noise, poor lighting, or physical distance that hinder communication. Since the problem described does not involve physical surroundings, an environmental barrier is not the correct answer here.

stem:
Which phases of the disaster cycle can be included in the recovery phase?
1. Response
2. Rehabilitation
3. Mitigation
4. Preparedness
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
Phases of the disaster cycle that can be included in the recovery phase are Disaster Mitigation and Disaster Preparedness.
Disaster Mitigation: 1. Means preventing the hazard from becoming a disaster. 2. Engineering modalities are used to prevent hazards.
Engineering Modalities: 1. Proper land use. 2. Improve building codes-Bulldings should be earthquake resistant. 3. Flood mitigation codes-Dams should be flood resistant.
Disaster Preparedness/Surge Capacity: 1. Surge capacity is the proper equipment of the country to manage disasters. 2. Overall capacity of the country to mariage disasters must be strengthened. 3. Country must have enough resources like manpower, money, material, technical expertise.



Other Options:
  • Options A, B and C: They are Incorrect as given above.

stem: Calculate the median of the following data set: 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8.
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
The median is the middle value of an ordered data set. Since there are 8 numbers, the median is the average of the 4th and 5th numbers (4 and 5). Thus, the median is (4+5)/2=4.5.



Other Options:
  • Options A, C and D: They are Incorrect as given above.

stem: The formula with the numerator being maternal deaths and the denominator being women of reproductive age represents which of the following?
subject: PSM, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
The maternal mortality rate (MMRate) is calculated as the number of maternal deaths per 100,000 women of reproductive age (usually defined as ages 15-49) in a given time period. It measures the risk of maternal death in the general population of women of reproductive age.



Other Options:
  • Option B: This ratio measures the number of maternal deaths per 100,000 live births in a given time period. It reflects the risk of death once a woman becomes pregnant, not the general population risk.
  • Option C: This rate measures the number of stillbirths and deaths within the first week of life per 1,000 total births (live births plus stillbirths). It does not relate to maternal deaths.
  • Option D: This term is not commonly used. If used, it might be mistaken for the perinatal mortality rate, but it does not apply to maternal deaths or reproductive-age women.

stem: What is the correct order of disaster management steps?

explanation:

Response: Actions taken to address the immediate needs and mitigate further damage.
Rehab: Short for rehabilitation, involving recovery efforts and rebuilding.
Mitigation: Long-term strategies to reduce the risk of future disasters.

stem: A 27 year old man eats outside due to traveling reasons. He presents to a doctor with abdominal pain for 5 days, along with a history of fever and bradycardia. Which test is used to confirm the diagnosis?
subject: Medicine, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Blood culture is the most reliable test to confirm the diagnosis in the early stages of typhoid fever, particularly within the first week of illness. Typhoid fever, caused by Salmonella typhi, is suspected due to the patient's symptoms of abdominal pain, fever, and bradycardia (a feature known as "relative bradycardia"). Blood culture can directly identify the bacteria responsible for the infection.



Other Options:
  • Option B: The Widal test detects antibodies against Salmonella antigens but is less specific and can yield false positives due to previous vaccination or exposure. It is typically used later in the course of the disease but is not as reliable for an Initial diagnosis compared to blood culture.
  • Option C: Stool culture can detect Salmonella in the stool, especially in the later stages of typhoid fever or in carriers, but it is less effective in the early stages of the illness when bacteria are more likely present in the bloodstream.
  • Option D: Urine culture is not typically used to diagnose typhoid fever. While Salmonella can occasionally be excreted in urine, this is not common during the acute phase of the disease.

stem: Which of the following substances should be eaten to prevent thiamine deficiency?
subject: PSM, Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
While refined rice and wheat are not the best sources of thiamine, whole grains and fortified products are better at preventing thiamine deficiency. Rice and wheat, when consumed in their whole form (le., brown rice and whole wheat), provide a better source of thiamine and can help in preventing thiamine deficiency compared to the other options listed.



Other Options:
  • Option B: While curd (yogurt) is a good source of various nutrients, including calcium and protein, it is not a significant source of thiamine. It does not play a major role in preventing thiamine deficiency.
  • Option C: Salads typically consist of vegetables and fruits, which can provide a range of vitamins and minerals. However, thiamine content in salads varies depending on the specific vegetables and fruits used. In general, salad is not a primary source of thiamine.
  • Option D: Aloo (potato) is commonly used in sabji (vegetable dishes) and is a good source of thiamine compared to some other foods. However, for a more substantial prevention of thiamine deficiency, other foods rich in thiamine should be included.

stem: Identify the index: Living Standards, Education, Health
subject: PSM
explanation:

The Human Development Index (HDI) is a composite statistic used to rank countries based on human development levels. It is calculated by assessing three key dimensions of human development: 1. Life Expectancy at Birth: Reflects the ability to live a long and healthy life. 2. Education: Measured by the mean years of schooling for adults aged 25 years or more and the expected years of schooling for children entering school. 3. Gross National Income (GNI) per Capita: Reflects the standard of living and economic conditions in a country, adjusted for purchasing power parity (PPP).



Other Options:

stem: A statistician wants to study the effects of medicine in three groups: humans, animals, and plants. He selects randomly out of these three groups. Which type of sampling is performed here?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
In the given scenario, the statistician is performing stratified random sampling. This approach involves dividing the population into distinct strata (humans, animals, and plants) and then selecting randomly from each stratum. This method ensures that each subgroup is represented in the study, allowing for a more detailed analysis of the effects of medicine across different types of organisms.



Other Options:
  • Option A: This method involves selecting individuals randomly from the entire population without considering any subgroups. In this scenario, simple random sampling would mean choosing any Individual from the combined groups of humans, animals, and plants without distinguishing between them.
  • Option B: This method involves selecting samples based on a fixed, periodic interval (e.g., every 10th Individual). Systematic sampling is not described in this scenario since the selection process does not involve intervals or systematic patterns.
  • Option D: This method involves dividing the population into clusters (groups) and then randomly selecting entire clusters for the study. The focus here is on the whole clusters rather than individual strata within those clusters.

stem: A group of 100 people was studied to assess the effect of diet modification on mean sugar levels before and after the intervention. Which statistical test is most appropriate to compare the mean sugar levels before and after the diet modification?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
The paired t-test is the most appropriate test for comparing the mean sugar levels before and after diet modification in the same group of individuals. This test accounts for the fact that the data are paired (pre- and post-diet modification) and assesses whether the mean difference is statistically significant.



Other Options:
  • Option B: This test is used for paired nominal data, specifically to analyze the difference in proportions for matched pairs, such as yes/no responses before and after an intervention. It is not appropriate for comparing means.
  • Option C: The chi-square test is used to analyze categorical data to determine if there is a significant association between variables. It is not suitable for comparing means, especially when dealing with continuous data like cholesterol levels.
  • Option D: This is a non-parametric test used to compare two related samples or repeated measurements on a single sample when the data does not follow a normal distribution. It is an alternative to the paired t-test when the data is not normally distributed.

stem: A female presents at 21 weeks of pregnancy due to rape. Which statement is correct regarding medical termination of pregnancy (MTP)?
options: Between 20-24 weeks, one doctor's opinion is required
Between 20-24 weeks, MTP is only done if it is life-saving for the mother
MTP can be done up to 24 weeks in this case
MTP can be done after 24 weeks
subject: PSM, Obstetrics and Gynaecology, FMT
explanation:
Solution
According to the amended MTP Act, 2021, MTP is permitted up to 24 weeks in cases of rape, with the opinion of two doctors.



Other Options:
  • Option A: Under the Medical Termination of Pregnancy (Amendment) Act, 2021, for pregnancies between 20-24 weeks, the opinion of two doctors is required, not just one. This applies to specific categories, including survivors of sexual assault.
  • Option B: While protecting the life of the mother is one consideration, MTP can also be performed during this period for other reasons, including if the pregnancy is a result of rape, or if there are significant fetal anomalies, without the need for the condition to be life-threatening to the mother.
  • Option D: MTP beyond 24 weeks is generally not permitted unless severe fetal anomalies are detected that are incompatible with life, or if there is a threat to the mother's life or health. This requires the approval of a medical board.

stem: Which of the following diseases has the maximum submerged portion of the iceberg?
subject: PSM, Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
Influenza has the maximum submerged portion of the Iceberg because a large number of infected Individuals may be asymptomatic or exhibit mild symptoms, resulting in underreporting and unrecognized cases. This large reservoir of mild or asymptomatic cases contributes significantly to the spread of the disease.



Other Options:
  • Option B: Like Influenza, chickenpox can have a large submerged portion where mild or atypical cases go unreported, especially in individuals with prior immunity or vaccination. However, most cases present with recognizable symptoms like rash.
  • Option C: Tetanus has a small submerged portion because it generally presents with noticeable and severe symptoms. It does not spread from person to person, so there is no asymptomatic carrier state contributing to its epidemiological iceberg.
  • Option D: Rabies has the least submerged portion of the iceberg because it is almost always symptomatic and progresses to severe outcomes once symptoms appear. Nearly all cases are identified due to the seriousness of the disease.

stem: The following instrument is used for the measurement of:
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
The above-given image shows a KATA thermometer.
Cooling Power of Air:
1. Determines the capacity of the atmosphere to dissipate the metabolic heat generated by humans.
2. Includes 3 components: Air temperature, Air humidity, and Air velocity.
3. Measured by a Kata thermometer.



Other Options:
  • Option B and D: Globe thermometer measures radiant heat and effective temperature.
  • Option C: The hygrometer measures air humidity.

stem: Given 500 live births, 12 stillbirths, and 9 deaths within the first 7 days, calculate the Early Neonatal Mortality Rate (ENMR):
subject: PSM, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
The Early Neonatal Mortality Rate (ENMR) is calculated as the number of deaths of live-born infants within the first 7 days of life per 1000 live births.
Formula:
ENMR = (Number of deaths in the first 7 days / Total number of live births) * 1000
Substituting the given values:
ENMR = (9 / 500) * 1000 = 18



Other Options:
  • Options B, C and D: are incorrect as given above.

stem: A child has papules on the extensor surfaces of the skin, which resolve with the administration of vitamins A and C and essential amino acids. What is your diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics, Dermatology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Phrynoderma is characterized by papular lesions on the extensor surfaces, which can improve with vitamin A supplementation. The resolution of symptoms with the administration of vitamins A and C, along with essential amino acids, is consistent with this diagnosis.



Other Options:
  • Option B: This is a condition resulting from vitamin D deficiency, leading to weak and soft bones in children. Symptoms include bone deformities and growth retardation, but it does not typically present with papules on the extensor surfaces.
  • Option C: This is a form of protein-energy malnutrition characterized by edema, Irritability, and skin changes. It usually Involves more generalized symptoms of malnutrition rather than isolated papules that resolve with vitamins and amino acids.
  • Option D: This is another form of severe malnutrition caused by a deficiency in caloric intake, leading to severe weight loss and muscle wasting. It does not present with papules but rather with severe overall wasting.

stem: Identify the image given:
subject: PSM, Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
The 'Sapna' campaign is linked to leprosy awareness in India, symbolizing hope and the possibility of a future free from leprosy-related stigma and disease.



Other Options:
  • Option A: This option may refer to a character or campaign related to HIV awareness, but it is not the correct association for the image related to leprosy.
  • Option C: The Meena campaign, supported by UNICEF, focuses on gender equality and education for girls in South Asia. It is not related to leprosy or the image in question.

stem: In a recent road traffic accident, the accused is a 14 years old boy but during medico legal examination, he claims to be 18 years old. Which joint X-ray should be done?
subject: FMT, Radiology, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
Knee and wrist X-rays are commonly used to assess skeletal maturity and bone age in medicolegal examinations. These joints are particularly useful for evaluating the maturity of the bones and growth plates, which helps in estimating the chronological age of an individual. The epiphyseal ossification patterns in the knee and wrist are well-documented and reliable Indicators of skeletal age.



Other Options:
  • Option A: X-rays of the head and shoulder are less useful for determining bone age because the skeletal maturity indicators are less distinct in these regions compared to the knee and wrist.
  • Option B: While the elbow and ankle can provide some information on bone age, they are not as commonly used or as reliable as the knee and wrist for this purpose. The elbow and ankle are not as definitive in assessing the maturity of growth plates.
  • Option D: The elbow and hip can be used to assess bone age, but they are not as commonly employed as the knee and wrist. The hip is less frequently used for this purpose because of variability in ossification patterns.

stem: Police brought a young person from the railway track with features of dry dilated pupil, dry skin, slurring of speech, altered sensorium. The poisoning is:
subject: FMT, Medicine, Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
Datura is a plant known for its anticholinergic properties. It can cause symptoms of anticholinergic toxicity, which include dry skin, dilated pupils (mydriasis), slurred speech, and altered sensorium. This is consistent with the clinical presentation described.



Other Options:
  • Option A: Morphine is an opioid and typically causes miosis (constricted pupils), not mydriasis. It can lead to respiratory depression and altered sensorium, but the dry skin and dilated pupils are not characteristic of morphine poisoning.
  • Option B: Cannabis can cause altered sensorium and slurred speech but is not associated with dry skin or dilated pupils. Cannabis effects are typically less severe and do not match the full clinical picture described.
  • Option D: Alcohol can cause altered sensorium and slurred speech, but it is not associated with dry skin or dilated pupils. Alcohol typically causes miosis and does not have the anticholinergic effects seen with Datura.

stem: A child was born after 7 months of father's death. The grandparents filed a case that the baby is not their son's. DNA is positive. The child is called:
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
A posthumous child is one who is born after the death of one or both parents. In this case, since the child was born after the father's death but is confirmed by DNA to be the biological child of the deceased father, the correct term is 'posthumous child'.



Other Options:
  • Option A: A suppositious child refers to a child who is falsely claimed to be the legitimate child of a deceased person, but this term is not commonly used in modern legal or medical contexts. It does not apply to the situation described.
  • Option B: A fabricated child is not a standard term used in legal or medical contexts. It suggests falsification but is not relevant in the context of a child's legitimacy or paternity.
  • Option D: An Illegitimate child is one born out of wedlock. However, since the issue in this case is related to the timing of birth relative to the father's death rather than marital status, 'illegitimate child' is not the most accurate term here.

stem: The dead body shows the following finding as in image. What is this case?
subject: FMT, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Smothering is characterized by the presence of bruises around the perioral region and nail abrasions around the lips and nose. Petechial hemorrhages are also commonly observed in smothering cases. This form of asphyxia occurs when an object or material covers the nose and mouth, obstructing the airways and preventing breathing.



Other Options:
  • Option A: In hanging cases, there is typically a ligature mark around the neck, and the pattern of petechiae is usually more prominent in the eyes and face rather than the perioral region. There are often no specific bruises around the lips or nail abrasions.
  • Option B: Throttling involves a deep bruise or contusion around the neck, commonly seen as a result of manual compression. This would not typically present with bruises specifically around the perioral region or nail abrasions.
  • Option C: Chaking (Choking) usually does not result in significant external trauma or bruising around the perioral area. Instead, it is characterized by signs of airway obstruction without specific bruises around the lips or petechial hemorrhages.

stem: A 24 years old woman with 27 weeks of gestation has been confirmed with some congenital anomalies during scan. Whose presence is not required for authorization of MTP?
subject: FMT, Obstetrics and Gynaecology
explanation:
Solution
A lawyer's presence is not required for the authorization of a Medical Termination of Pregnancy (MTP) for congenital anomalies. The primary requirement is the involvement of a medical team with specific specialists. The lawyer's role typically comes into play in legal proceedings or when there are legal challenges, but not in the medical decision-making process for MTP.



Other Options:
  • Option A: Required. An obstetrician is essential for evaluating and managing the medical aspects of the pregnancy, Including assessing the viability and risks Involved with the continuation or termination of the pregnancy.
  • Option C: Required. A pediatrician's input is crucial when congenital anomalies are involved, as they provide insight into the nature and severity of the anomalies, which helps in making informed decisions about the MTP.
  • Option D: Required. A sonologist, who performs and interprets ultrasound scans, plays a critical role in diagnosing congenital anomalies and assessing the gestational age of the fetus. This information is vital for decision-making regarding MTP.

stem: Examination of a suspicious stain found at a crime site shows the following finding. The test is:
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
The Barberios test is used for the detection of semen and is characterized by the formation of yellow, needle-shaped crystals when a stain is treated with certain reagents. This test is specific for identifying seminal fluid.



Other Options:
  • Option B: The Florence test detects the presence of blood by forming yellow, needle-shaped crystals. However, in practice, It's often referred to as the Florence Reaction, which is not commonly used to identify semen.
  • Option C: The Teichman test is also used for blood detection but is characterized by the formation of rhomboid-shaped crystals, not needle-shaped ones.
  • Option D: The Takayama test detects blood and forms pink or reddish-brown crystals, not yellow needle-shaped crystals.

stem: Recording of evidence of a female in sexual assault in court is done in:
subject: FMT
explanation:
Solution
In cases involving sexual assault, closed court proceedings are used to ensure the privacy and protection of the victim. These proceedings are not open to the public, which helps maintain the victim's confidentiality and reduce the emotional impact of the court process. Closed court sessions help protect the victim from public scrutiny and potential re-traumatization.



Other Options:
  • Option A: Open court proceedings are accessible to the public and can expose the victim to unnecessary stress and scrutiny. Such settings do not provide the privacy needed for sensitive cases like sexual assault.
  • Option C: While a hearing might be conducted from a different location for logistical reasons (e.g., via video link), this is not specifically designed to protect the privacy of the victim in the same way that closed court proceedings do.
  • Option D: Although on-camera recordings may be used to record testimony, this does not replace the need for closed court proceedings.

stem: Sequence of rigor mortis is:
subject: FMT, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
Rigor mortis, or postmortem rigidity, typically starts in the smaller muscles and progresses to the larger muscle groups. The sequence generally begins with the muscles of the face and neck, followed by the upper limbs, and then extends to the trunk and lower limbs. Therefore, rigor mortis progresses from the head and upper parts of the body downwards towards the feet.



Other Options:
  • Option A: Rigor mortis does not progress from the center of the body to the periphery. It starts in the head and moves towards the feet, not the central parts to the outer extremities.
  • Option C: Rigor mortis does not start in the feet and move upwards to the head. It starts in the head and progresses downwards towards the feet.
  • Option D: Rigor mortis does not occur simultaneously in all parts of the body. It progresses in a specific sequence, starting with the head and upper body, then moving downwards.

stem: A 25 year old man wears a female dress and high heels and gets pleasure by doing so. He was not attracted to the same gender. This condition is:
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
Transvestic fetishism involves wearing clothing traditionally associated with the opposite sex for sexual arousal. It is characterized by deriving sexual pleasure from cross-dressing, without necessarily having an attraction to the same gender. The person in this scenario finds pleasure in wearing female clothing and high heels but is not attracted to the same sex.



Other Options:
  • Option B: Voyeurism Involves gaining sexual pleasure from watching others who are unaware that they are being watched, particularly in intimate or private situations. It is not related to cross-dressing or deriving pleasure from wearing the clothing of the opposite sex.
  • Option C: Sexual dysphoria (more commonly referred to as gender dysphoria) is the distress or discomfort that may occur when an individual's gender Identity does not align with their assigned sex at birth. This condition involves a disconnect between one's experienced gender and the gender assigned at birth, not the act of cross-dressing for pleasure.
  • Option D: Gay orientation refers to an attraction to individuals of the same gender. The scenario described does not involve sexual attraction to the same sex but rather involves deriving pleasure from wearing clothing typically associated with the opposite sex.

stem: A 3-month-old child is brought to the ophthalmology OPD with the following presentation. What is the ideal management?
subject: Ophthalmology, Pediatrics, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
In this situation, the image reveals bilateral congenital cataracts, meaning both eyes are affected. If not treated promptly, this condition can result in Irreversible vision loss due to amblyopia. For bilateral cataracts, surgery is typically performed after the child reaches one month of age, so immediate surgery is necessary in this case.



Other Options:
  • Options A, C and D: They are incorrect, as explained above.

stem: A 34 year old woman patient presents with a gradual loss of night vision and peripheral vision. What is the most likely diagnosis based on fundoscopy findings?
subject: Ophthalmology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The patient's presentation and presence of bony spicules, pale waxy disc, and attenuated arteries are suggestive of retinitis pigmentosa.



Other Options:
  • Options B, C and D: They do not exhibit the fundoscopy findings given in the question.

stem: Which muscle is implicated in ptosis in Horner syndrome?
subject: Ophthalmology, Anatomy, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
Ptosis in Horner syndrome is due to the paralysis of the Müller's muscle, a smooth muscle responsible for a minor portion of upper eyelid retraction.



Other Options:
  • Options B, C and D: They are not responsible for causing ptosis in Horner's syndrome.

stem: During the Worth 4 Dot Test, a 37 year old diabetic patient sees only three green dots. What is the likely diagnosis?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
The Worth 4 Dot Test is used to assess binocular vision. Right eye suppression is diagnosed when the patient reports seeing only three green dots.



Other Options:
  • Option B: A patient with left eye suppression will only see two red dots.
  • Option C and D: A patient with diplopia will see five dots - two red and three green dots.

Solution
The image shows scarring in the upper limbus, which is referred to as Herbert's pits. It is seen in the chronic cicatricial stage of trachoma.

Other Options:

  • Option C: Pannus
  • Option D: Corneal dystrophy

    Microcystic/Map dot/Fingerprint Dystrophy:

    Meesmann dystrophy:

    Reis-Bucklers Corneal dystrophy

stem: A 27 year old male patient presents with sudden diminution of vision, difficulty looking towards the light, and circumcorneal congestion with a hypopyon. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Ophthalmology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The clinical presentation of the patient is suggestive of acute anterior uveitis. The presence of a hypopyon is often seen in conditions like Behcet disease, which can typically cause non-granulomatous anterior uveitis.



Other Options:
  • Options A, B and C: They do not present with a hypopyon.

stem: A 50 year old female patient presents with decreased vision three years after surgery and exhibits this condition. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Ophthalmology, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
Posterior Capsular Opacification can occur after cataract surgery when residual lens epithelial cells migrate and proliferate on the posterior capsule. Elschnig Pearls are clusters of proliferating lens epithelial cells that form a circular, pearl-like opacity on the posterior capsule, contributing to the visual disturbances (as seen in the given Image).



Other Options:
  • Options A, B and C: They do not exhibit Elschnig pearls.

stem: Identify the visual field defect:
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
The given image shows blind spot enlargement.

Other Options:
  • Option A: Altitudinal defect

  • Option B: Roenne's nasal step

  • Option D: Arcuate scotoma

stem: A young patient presents to the outpatient department with the following findings. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Ophthalmology, Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
Limbal dermoids are congenital benign tumors that cause astigmatism, encroachment on the visual axis, and fatty component infiltration into the cornea. They consist of overgrowths of epidermal appendages, connective tissues, skin, fat, sweat glands, lacrimal glands, and neurologic tissues located partly over the cornea and partly over the sclera.



Other Options:
  • Option A: A pterygium is a fibrovascular growth of the conjunctiva that extends onto the cornea.
  • Option C: A pyogenic granuloma is a red, fleshy, vascular lesion that usually arises after trauma or surgery.
  • Option D: A pinguecula is a yellowish, slightly raised lesion on the conjunctiva, usually near the limbus.

stem: A child presents with leukocoria and an intraocular mass. Microscopic examination reveals rosettes with scanty cytoplasm and the mass is positive for synaptophysin and chromogranin. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Ophthalmology, Pediatrics, Pathology
explanation:
Solution
The presence of an intraocular mass in a child with leukocoria is suggestive of retinoblastoma. Flexner-Wintersteiner rosettes and Homer Wright rosettes are characteristic histopathological features of retinoblastoma. It is positive for synaptophysin, NSE, and chromogranin.



Other Options:
  • Options B, C and D: They do not align with the features described in the question.

stem: Which of the following statements is false regarding the condition shown in the image?
options: Management corresponds to an improvement in the thyrotoxic state
It is not seen in 10% of patients with hyperthyroidism
subject: Ophthalmology, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The given image shows Kocher's sign, which is seen in thyroid eye disease. The resection of overacting muscle LPS and resecting of underacting muscle will help to resolve the patient's symptoms, not just the management of the thyrotoxic state.



Other Options:
  • Options A, C and D: They are true about thyroid eye diseases.

stem: A 22 year old female singer is finding difficulty in raising the pitch of her voice. On examination, her vocal cords were depressed and bowing. Which of the following muscles is involved?
subject: ENT, Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
The cricothyroid muscle is crucial for raising the pitch of the voice. It is a tensor muscle of the vocal cords. When there is dysfunction or paralysis of the cricothyroid muscle, the vocal cords are not properly tensed, leading to difficulty in raising the pitch.



Other Options:
  • Option B and D: Interarytenoid and Lateral arytenoid are adductors of the vocal cord.
  • Option C: Posterior cricoarytenoid is an abductor of the vocal cords.

stem: A 40-year-old woman was recently diagnosed with right vestibular neuronitis. What findings are expected during the head Impulse test in this patient?
options: When the head is turned to the left, the eyeballs move to the left with nystagmus.
When the head is turned to the left, the eyeballs move to the right with nystagmus.
When the head is turned to the right, the eyeballs move to the left with nystagmus.
When the head is turned to the right, the eyeballs move to the right with nystagmus.
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
The head Impulse test is a clinical maneuver used to assess the function of the vestibulo-ocular reflex (VOR), which stabilizes gaze during head movement.
In a patient with right vestibular neuronitis, the right vestibular nerve is impaired.
When the head is turned to the right:
1. The right vestibular system fails to generate the appropriate signal to maintain gaze.
2. The eyes initially move with the head to the right (which is abnormal) and then perform a quick corrective movement (catch-up saccade) to the left to refocus on the target.



Other Options:
  • Options A, B and D: They are incorrect, as explained above.

stem: Impaired gag reflex is caused by a lesion in which of the following cranial nerves?
subject: Anatomy, ENT, Medicine
explanation:
Solution
The sensory supply of the oropharynx is via the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX), and the motor supply is via the vagus nerve (CN X). So, any lesion in these nerves will lead to an impaired gag reflex.



Other Options:
  • Options A, B and D: They are incorrect, as explained above.

stem: A 47 year old male patient with prolonged intubation complained of respiratory distress. Which of the following staging systems is used to stage the condition shown in the image?
subject: ENT, Anesthesia
explanation:
Solution
The Cotton-Meyer classification is specifically designed to stage laryngotracheal stenosis, particularly the narrowing of the airway after intubation or tracheostomy. It grades the stenosis based on the percentage of the lumen that is narrowed.



Other Options:
  • Options B, C and D: They are not used to classify subglottic stenosis.

stem: A 13-year-old child with a history of recurrent bleeding undergoes an endoscopic examination, which is given below. What is the investigation of choice (IOC) for this condition?
subject: ENT, Pediatrics, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
The Image shows a globular pink mass in the posterior part of the nasal cavity, and the septum is being pushed to the opposite side. These findings are suggestive of juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma, and the investigation of choice is a CECT scan.



Other Options:
  • Options B, C and D: They are not the IOC for JNAF.

stem: Based on the provided chest X-ray, which of the following procedures would be most challenging to perform?
subject: ENT, Anesthesia, Radiology
explanation:
Solution
The given image shows the deviation of the trachea towards the right side, which will make it very difficult to perform a tracheostomy.



Other Options:
  • Options B, C and D: They are not difficult to perform in a patient with a deviated trachea.

stem: Which of the following signs can be appreciated in the given X-ray?
subject: Radiology, Pediatrics, Surgery
explanation:
Solution
A double ring sign is observed on the given X-ray and is characteristic of a button battery aspiration. The double ring appearance is due to the unique structure of a button battery, where the edge of the battery gives a double contour.
Other Options:
  • Option A: The steeple sign is associated with croup (laryngotracheobronchitis).
  • Option B: The string sign is observed in pyloric stenosis.

  • Option C: The thumb sign is associated with epiglottitis.

stem: A 22 year old male patient presents with a history of ear discharge. On examination, a small perforation is observed in the antero-inferior segment of the tympanic membrane. What is the most likely result of Rinne's test?
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
In this patient, the small perforation in the tympanic membrane suggests a conductive hearing loss. In conductive hearing loss, bone conduction (BC) is better than air conduction (AC), which impedes sound waves from reaching the Inner ear through air conduction. Thus, the Rinne test will be negative (BC>AC).



Other Options:
  • Options A, C and D: They are incorrect, as explained above.

stem: Which sinus drainage is obstructed in the image provided?
subject: Radiology, ENT
explanation:
Solution
The given radiograph shows blockage in both the maxillary sinuses, predominantly in the right maxillary sinus.



Other Options:
  • Options B, C and D: They are not obstructed in the given X-ray.
